title: Vitamin D & respiratory infections (3 turns · conflict + subgroup refinement + shared source)
note: Real, checkable. Exercises: a source co-cited across turns (Martineau → shared node), a contradiction (VITAL vs blanket claim), and a scope refinement (effect conditional on baseline status).

trace.content: Does vitamin D supplementation prevent acute respiratory infections?

arguments.q: vitamin D supplementation acute respiratory infection meta-analysis

trace.content: Martineau et al. 2017, BMJ — an individual-participant-data meta-analysis of 25 randomized controlled trials (~11,000 participants) found vitamin D supplementation reduced the risk of acute respiratory tract infection (adjusted odds ratio 0.88), with the strongest protective effect in participants with baseline 25(OH)D below 25 nmol/L (profoundly deficient).

A 2017 BMJ individual-participant-data meta-analysis (Martineau) found vitamin D supplementation modestly reduces acute respiratory infections overall, with the largest benefit in profoundly deficient individuals.
But didn't a large trial find no effect?

arguments.q: VITAL trial vitamin D respiratory infection no effect replete

trace.content: The VITAL trial (large RCT, ~25,000 US adults, the great majority vitamin-D-replete at baseline) reported that vitamin D3 2000 IU/day did NOT reduce the incidence of acute respiratory infections compared with placebo.
The VITAL trial found no overall effect in a largely vitamin-D-replete population, which contradicts the blanket claim that vitamin D prevents respiratory infections for everyone.

These reconcile via baseline status: the benefit concentrates in deficient people (Martineau) while a replete population (VITAL) shows none, so the protective effect is conditional on vitamin D deficiency, not universal.
So what's the bottom line for a healthy adult?

Martineau et al. 2017, BMJ — an individual-participant-data meta-analysis of 25 randomized controlled trials (~11,000 participants) found vitamin D supplementation reduced the risk of acute respiratory tract infection (adjusted odds ratio 0.88), with the strongest protective effect in participants with baseline 25(OH)D below 25 nmol/L (profoundly deficient).

Bottom line: vitamin D supplementation reduces acute respiratory infections mainly in deficient individuals; for a vitamin-D-replete healthy adult, routine supplementation for infection prevention is not supported by the evidence.
